text: Our patient is placed under general anesthesia with a double lumen tube and is positioned in the right lateral decubitus position.
Our approach for this case is through a left uniportal VATS incision that was placed in the sixth intracoastal space.
It is approximately four centimeters in size.

During the operation, we encountered flimsy adhesions onto the left upper lobe, and later on in the operation when we start to visualize the left lower lobe, you will see the dense and fibrotic adhesions associated with chronic inflammation.
Now you get to see the left lower lobe.
As you can see, it's quite destroyed and it's on the right side of the screen.
We're now creating the fissure through a mixture of blunt dissection, shard dissection, as well as stapling.
The left lower lobe is quite adherent to the mediastinum and we spent some time dissecting out the inferior pulmonary vein to get control and finally transect it.
The pulmonary artery branches are exposed through the fissure.
There's quite a bit of adhesions in this area and it takes some time and patience to dissect out the basilar artery.
While examining the bronchus, we could see that there is a secondary structure adherent to it.
Initially, it wasn't clear if this was the superior segmental bronchus or not.
It later turned out to be the superior segmental pulmonary artery.
It is quite difficult to appreciate because of the dense adhesions and shows some of the technical issues with performing surgery for bronchiectasis.
The bronchus is clamped with the EndoGI stapler and the lung is inflated under direct vision and you can see clearly that the left upper lobe and lingula are free.
We finally perform an underwater test to make sure our staple line is intact before placing our chest tube.
